mnemonics.title: P.U.M.P. Lemma
mnemonics.content: P - Pumping length 'n' exists | U - Use any string w ≥ n | M - Maintain | P - Partition into xyz. Three conditions: |xy| ≤ n (pump within first n symbols), |y| > 0 (y is non-empty), xyⁱz ∈ L for all i ≥ 0 (pumped string stays in language)

mnemonics.title: C.L.A.S.P. Closures
mnemonics.content: Regular languages are Closed under: L - Union (R1 ∪ R2), A - Intersection (R1 ∩ R2), S - Complement (Σ* - R), P - Product/Concatenation (R1R2), * - Kleene Star (R*), H - Homomorphism, I⁻¹ - Inverse Homomorphism

mnemonics.title: E.D.C.M. Decision Properties
mnemonics.content: E - Emptiness: Test if L(M) = ∅ (is any accept state reachable from start?) | D - Disjointness: Test if L(M1) ∩ L(M2) = ∅ (product automaton approach) | C - Containment: Test if L(M1) ⊆ L(M2) (check L(M1) ∩ L(M2)' = ∅) | M - Membership: Test if w ∈ L(M) (simulation on DFA)

mnemonics.title: M.N.R. Theorem
mnemonics.content: Myhill-Nerode Theorem: Number of equivalence classes = Minimum DFA states. L is regular iff: R - Right-invariant equivalence relation is of finite index (partitions finite strings into finite groups) | E - Equivalent states distinguishable by some string

mnemonics.title: D.E.A.D. Minimization
mnemonics.content: DFA Minimization Algorithm: D - Delete unreachable states | E - Equivalence classes initially: accepting vs non-accepting | A - Adjust: split classes if distinguishable (different outputs for same input) | D - Done: when no splits occur; each class becomes one state

mnemonics.title: R.E.G.E.X. Proof
mnemonics.content: To prove closure under Intersection: R1 ∩ R2 = (R1' ∪ R2')' (De Morgan's Law). Use Product Construction: States = (p,q) from M1 × M2, Accept if both p and q are accepting, Transitions on each symbol simultaneously

acronyms.usage: A language recognized by some DFA/NFA/regex
Used to prove a language is NOT regular by showing it cannot be pumped
Theorem defining regular language via equivalence classes
Complete, deterministic transition function; used in minimization
Multiple transitions possible; equivalent to DFA for regular languages
NOT closed under intersection (unlike RL); used in contrast with regular properties

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a balloon (string w) being pumped - the 'y' portion is the balloon's air chamber that repeats/expands. The 'x' part stays fixed at start, 'y' is the repeatable middle, 'z' is the tail. Visualize y being stretched (pumped) while x and z remain constant.
Think of a chessboard - each square is a state pair (p,q). Move like a knight on both boards simultaneously. Win (accept) only when you land on winning squares on BOTH boards at the same time.

Imagine a sorting machine with finite bins. Strings go into bins - all strings in same bin behave identically from that point forward. If bins are finite, language is regular. Infinite bins = non-regular.
Use a table with accepting states vs non-accepting states (marked X for distinguishable). Fill in pairs that differ on each input symbol. Iteratively mark more pairs. Unmarked pairs can be merged.

Think of a translator. Each input symbol gets mapped to a string (like English→French). Run the original machine on translated string. If original accepts, translated accepts - homomorphism preserves regularity.

analogies.analogy: Like a song with chorus - the 'y' is the repeating chorus. No matter how many times (i) you repeat the chorus, the song (language) remains valid. Non-regular languages are like songs where chorus length must change each verse.

Like grouping students by their future behavior. Two students (strings) are equivalent if, from any point (suffix added), they get the same result (acceptance/rejection). Regular languages have finite 'personality types'.

Like merging duplicate pages in a book. If Page A and Page B always lead to identical remaining story regardless of what happens next, they're redundant - merge them into one page. Final book (DFA) has minimum pages.
Like checking if there's any path from start to a goal in a maze. Do a graph search (BFS/DFS) from start state. If goal (accept state) unreachable, language is empty.

Like checking if two different train schedules (DFAs) take you to the same final destination for every possible route. Use product construction - if there's any route where one accepts and other rejects, they're NOT equivalent.

Like proving a number is irrational by assuming rational and reaching contradiction. Assume regular → must satisfy PL → show contradiction → language is non-regular. Example: L = {aⁿbⁿ | n≥0} - pumping y creates mismatch between a's and b's.
